Update from 29 September 2022 - How are ECTS credits counted when I resume my studies? If you resume your studies within the same curriculum, the counting of the ECTS credits continues after resumption. If you interrupt your studies and later resume your studies in a succeeding curriculum, or if you change a teaching subject in your combination, your previously obtained ECTS credits must be recognised to ensure that they count towards the minimum number of credits. If you still need ECTS credits for the minimum number of credits, you have to obtain them within the remaining time. - What happens if I do not obtain the minimum number of credits? If you do not obtain the minimum number of credits (16 ECTS credits) for a degree programme within the first four semesters, your admission to the degree programme expires on 31 October or 31 March, depending on whether the fourth semester is the summer or the winter semester. You will be excluded from this programme for four semesters. Once four semesters have passed, you can apply

Achievements completed in complementary degree programmes are not recognised for the minimum number of credits. - I am enrolled in the bachelor's programme in Teacher Education. Do I have to obtain the minimum number of credits for each teaching subject separately? What happens if I change my teaching subject? In the bachelor’s programme in Teacher Education, you have to obtain the minimum number of credits for both teaching subjects and the pedagogical part in total. You do not have to obtain the minimum number of credits for each teaching subject separately. If one teaching subject in your combination changes, as long as one of the two teaching subjects remains the same, the semesters continue to be counted for the new combination. Both newly obtained ECTS credits and ECTS credits recognised in due time are counting towards the ECTS credits for the new combination. If you already obtained the minimum number of credits for the old combination, you do not have to obtain them for the new one again. If you do not obtain the minimum number of credits in due time, you

text: your teaching subjects with the subject of your complementary degree programme. You have to obtain the minimum number of credits within the remaining fourth semester of the combination programme. Exams completed in the complementary degree programme count, if you completed them within the first four semesters of the combination programme and if you apply for recognition in due time (see FAQ: “Are recognised achievements accepted for the minimum number of credits?”). - Are recognised achievements accepted for the minimum number of credits? Whether a recognised achievement is accepted for the minimum number of credits depends on the date on which the achievement (= initial achievement) was completed. The initial achievement must have been completed within the period relevant for the minimum number of credits from the first start of a degree programme and its recognition must have been approved. Only then does the recognised achievement count towards the minimum number of credits. Therefore, apply for recognition at your responsible StudiesServiceCenter well in advance. Note that it might take up to two months until a decision is taken and that the complaints procedure in case of a negative decision also takes time. If you have completed a course/exam multiple times, only the date of the last examination result counts. - I study at least one of my two teaching subjects at a university of art in Vienna. text: time. If you have completed a course/exam multiple times, only the date of the last examination result counts. - I study at least one of my two teaching subjects at a university of art in Vienna. What are the requirements for my studies? If at least one subject in a teacher education combination is completed at a university of art in Vienna, the respective university of art is responsible for calculating the ECTS credits. The ECTS credits are calculated over the entire duration of the degree programme. Information about exams at the University of Vienna will be obtained if necessary. If students have completed less than 16 ECTS credits by the end of the fourth semester (deadline 31 October for the summer semester/31 March for the winter semester), it is up to the respective university of art to exclude the student from the degree programme after reviewing all information. ECTS credits obtained at the University of Vienna are displayed in [u:space](https://uspace.univie.ac.at/web/studium). - Are there any programs that support me in my studies and in achieving the minimum number of credits? This is how you can progress in your studies: - Plan your semester. - Study continuously. - Use the study materials on Moodle. - Form study groups. - Make us of tutorials. - Take exams. [Information about Semesterplanning](/en/semester-planning/) Make use of the [services for students](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/en/services-for-students/) to achieve your goal. The

The University supports you by offering: - In your first semester: [STEOP mentoring](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/en/services-for-students/your-first-semester/steop-mentoring/) - Reflect on, and adapt, your own learning approach: [Learning camp](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/en/services-for-students/during-the-degree-programme/learning-camp/) - Improve your academic writing for paper or thesis: [Writing mentoring](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/en/services-for-students/during-the-degree-programme/writing-mentoring/), [writing consultation](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/en/services-for-students/during-the-degree-programme/writing-consultation/) - [Helpful handouts and blog posts (german)](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/angebote-fuer-studierende/ressourcen/handouts-leitfaeden-und-infomaterial/) As usual, we keep you informed by e-mail, on the websites and via the social media channels of the University of Vienna. title: Registration for courses and examinations
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text:
The following page provides instructions on how to register for courses and exams. In order to register for courses and exams, you need an active [u:account](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/uaccount/) as well as a valid [admission](/en/admission-procedure/) to a degree programme at the University of Vienna.

[Registration for courses](#c254735)
[Registration for exams](#c254738)

- For information about how to plan your schedule, please go to the website [Planning your semester](/en/semester-planning/).
- If you have any questions about the registration (registration periods, limited numbers of study places), please contact your [studies service center (SSC)/studies service unit (SSS)](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/).
- The [ABC of terminology](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1) provides explanations on the university terminology.

[### Registration for courses with non-continuous assessment](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/registration-for-courses-with-non-continuous-assessment/)

[### Registration for courses with continuous assessment](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/registration-for-courses-with-continuous-assessment/)

text: / - Step 1: Check your record of examinations and/or semester planner You can find the record of examinations in u:space under the tab "Studies". It is organised according to the structure of your curriculum. This way, you can see at a glance which courses you have already completed, which achievements you have to complete next and what requirements you have to fulfil for them. You can find the semester planner in u:space under the tab "Courses". It shows you the courses currently offered and provides you with a calendar to help you plan your semester. Attention: Please always check the [curriculum of your degree programme](https://senat.univie.ac.at/curricularkommission/curricula). The record of examinations in u:space supports you in planning, but you have to select the correct study plan point yourself on the basis of your curriculum when registering for the course or exam. - Step 2: Select an achievement from your curriculum Search for modules for which you still have to complete courses (red and orange traffic light symbol) in your record of examinations in u:space. After that with the help of the record of examinations and by looking at your [curriculum](https://senat.univie.ac.at/curricularkommission/curricula), determine for which curriculum point (module) you are still missing a course / exam. To find out which courses you can attend for the curriculum point you have not yet completed, either click on "Link to u:find" at the book icon

To find out which courses you can attend for the curriculum point you have not yet completed, either click on "Link to u:find" at the book icon or search directly in the Course Directory [u:find](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/en/vvz.html) for courses suitable for the curriculum point. Alternatively, you can search for modules that you have not yet completed in the semester planner in u:space (completed = green tick). There you will find all the courses currently offered for the module. Use your [curriculum](https://senat.univie.ac.at/curricularkommission/curricula) to check for which curriculum point the course can be recognised. - Step 3: Select course in u:find Click on the courses available for completing the selected achievement in the Course Directory [u:find](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/de/index.html). You will get detailed information about the course type, number of ECTS credits, registration and de-registration periods, course dates, contents, Moodle, lecturers, etc. If you click on “Register/Deregister for this course” you will be automatically forwarded to u:space where you can complete your registration. - Step 4: Register for a course You can only register for courses within the relevant registration period. Certain courses may have a limited number of study places. As long as you register within the relevant registration period, the exact moment of your registration has no influence on the allocation of study places (no “first come, first served” principle). title: Registration for courses and examinations
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text: Then select any preferred groups (parallel courses). For courses with a limited number of study places, you will have to allocate points/preferences. Click on “Register” to complete the registration. As soon as the registration period has ended, the system will allocate the places on the courses. Registrations are organised according to the specifications of the curricula (e.g. [points system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408), [preference system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741407) or [recommended path of study](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/recommended-path-of-study/). Places will be allocated accordingly. Next, the status of your registration will be updated in u:space and you will receive an information e-mail on your u:account e-mail address. Possible registration status before the allocation of study places: - Prerequisite currently not met (Voraussetzung noch nicht erfüllt) - Allocation by registration deadline (Zuteilung nach Anmeldefrist) Possible registration status after the allocation of study places: - Registered (angemeldet) - On waiting list (auf Warteliste) - Alternative place available (Alternativplatz verfügbar) - In process (in Bearbeitung) - Closed (geschlossen) - No place received (keinen Platz erhalten) The ABC of terminology provides further information on the [registration status](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418). You can find an overview of your registered courses under registration overview in u:space. The deregistration from a course is done via the registration overview. #### ABC of terminology ##### Registering for courses - [Course](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741378) - [Record of examinations](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration deadline](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration status](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) [### Registration phases for courses with continuous assessment](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/registration-for-courses-with-continuous-assessment/registration-phases-for-courses-with-continuous-assessment/)
embed_text:

Then select any preferred groups (parallel courses). For courses with a limited number of study places, you will have to allocate points/preferences. Click on “Register” to complete the registration. As soon as the registration period has ended, the system will allocate the places on the courses. Registrations are organised according to the specifications of the curricula (e.g. [points system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408), [preference system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741407) or [recommended path of study](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/recommended-path-of-study/). Places will be allocated accordingly. Next, the status of your registration will be updated in u:space and you will receive an information e-mail on your u:account e-mail address. Possible registration status before the allocation of study places: - Prerequisite currently not met (Voraussetzung noch nicht erfüllt) - Allocation by registration deadline (Zuteilung nach Anmeldefrist) Possible registration status after the allocation of study places: - Registered (angemeldet) - On waiting list (auf Warteliste) - Alternative place available (Alternativplatz verfügbar) - In process (in Bearbeitung) - Closed (geschlossen) - No place received (keinen Platz erhalten) The ABC of terminology provides further information on the [registration status](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418). You can find an overview of your registered courses under registration overview in u:space. The deregistration from a course is done via the registration overview. #### ABC of terminology ##### Registering for courses - [Course](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741378) - [Record of examinations](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration deadline](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [Registration status](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) [### Registration phases for courses with continuous assessment](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/registration-for-courses-with-continuous-assessment/registration-phases-for-courses-with-continuous-assessment/)

title: Registration for courses and examinations
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text: / - Step 1: Search examination on u:find You can see the details about the exam (e.g. date, time, venue, examiner(s), alternative exam dates) on the Course Directory [u:find](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/en/index.html). Click on “Register/Deregister for this exam”. You will be forwarded to u:space, where you can complete your registration. Please click on the following link if you need [help in using u:find](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/en/help.html). - Step 2: Register for the examination You can only register for exams within the relevant registration period. You can check the registration period on u:find. In u:space you have to select your degree programme and the respective curriculum point (module). (If there are several curriculum points available, select the correct one as it is also listed in the Course Directory u:find. Otherwise your grade will be assigned to the wrong module in the record of examinations). Click on “Register” to complete the registration. Now, your registration has the [registration status](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) “Registered"(Angemeldet) in u:space. If you do not meet all the necessary requirements (e.g. examinations from the introductory and orientation period) for taking the exam, your registration status will be "Prerequisite currently not met". In the details you will find the missing requirements. In this case, you are not yet able to register for the exam. If you meet the requirements by the end of the registration deadline, your registration will be considered in the allocation process. For exams with

title: Registration for courses and examinations
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text: the missing requirements. In this case, you are not yet able to register for the exam. If you meet the requirements by the end of the registration deadline, your registration will be considered in the allocation process. For exams with limited number of participants each directorate of studies (in German: Studienprogrammleitung, SPL) determines **how places are allocated** in its degree programmes. Places are allocated using either the [point allocation system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741407), the [preference system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408), or the [recommended path of study](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/recommended-path-of-study/). In the registration overview in u:space you can deregister from the exam. If you have any questions about registration/de-registration, please contact the [studies service center/studies service unit](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for your degree programme. #### ABC of terminology ##### Registering for exams - [Exam](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741382) - [Registration](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [u:find](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741425) [## You've got further questions?](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) [You get further information at the StudiesServiceCenter (SSC) or StudiesServiceUnit (SSS) of your degree programme.](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/)

Places are allocated using either the [point allocation system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741407), the [preference system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408), or the [recommended path of study](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/recommended-path-of-study/). If you have any questions about registration/de-registration, please contact the [studies service center/studies service unit](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for your degree programme. #### ABC of terminology ##### Registering for exams - [Exam](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741382) - [Registration](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418) - [u:find](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741425) [## You've got further questions?](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) [You get further information at the StudiesServiceCenter (SSC) or StudiesServiceUnit (SSS) of your degree programme.](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/)

The registration for courses with non-continuous assessment is not subject to any conditions, such as attendance or participation. Nevertheless a registration is recommended.

The registration period runs over the entire semester. In addition to a registration you are automatically registered for the respective Moodle course.

text: ## / - Step 1: Check your record of examinations and/or semester planner You can find the record of examinations in u:space under the tab “Studies”. It is organised according to the structure of your curriculum. This way, you can see at a glance which courses you have already completed, which achievements you have to complete next and what requirements you have to fulfil for them. You can find the semester planner in u:space under the tab “Courses”. It shows you the courses currently offered and provides you with a calendar to help you plan your semester. Attention: Please always check the [curriculum of your degree programme](https://senat.univie.ac.at/curricularkommission/curricula). The record of examinations in u:space supports you in planning, but you have to select the correct curriculum point yourself on the basis of your curriculum when registering for the course or exam. - Step 2: Select an achievement from your curriculum Search for modules for which you still have to complete courses (red and orange traffic light symbol) in your record of examinations in u:space. After that with the help of the record of examinations and by looking at your [curriculum](https://senat.univie.ac.at/curricularkommission/curricula), determine for which curriculum point (module) you are still missing a course / exam. To find out which courses you can attend for the curriculum point you have not yet completed, either click on "Link to u:find" at the book icon

title: Registration for courses with continuous assessment
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Registration for courses with continuous assessment
text:
Seminars, exercises e.g. are courses with continous assessment (German: prüfungsimmanente Lehrveranstaltung, pi LV). Courses with continuous assessment require a registration. In courses with continuous assessment attendance and other requirements are compulsory. The registration deadlines run in the weeks before the semester starts, i.e. in September for the winter semester and in February for the summer semester. If you are registered for a course with continuous assessment and are absent from the first unit without having a good reason, you will be deregistered.

With the registration to a course, you are automatically registered for the respective Moodle course.

- Some courses have a limited number of participants.
- Places are allocated in different ways, which are determined by each [directorate of studies](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/abc-of-terminology/#c273314) (in German: Studienprogrammleitung, SPL)
- Either through the [point allocation system](#c600152), the [preference system](#c600156) or the [recommended path of study](/en/registration-for-coursesexaminations/recommended-path-of-study/).
- Students on the [waiting list](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/abc-of-terminology/#c273323) will be accepted if course places become available. In case you are on the waiting list, please attend the first unit of the course, as there may be free places.

For some pi LV groups are offered. **Groups** are parallel offers of a course, which can differ in name, place and time. When registering, you can select several groups and then prioritize them. title: Registration for courses with continuous assessment
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Registration for courses with continuous assessment
text:
- [Allocation of places in courses and exams](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c721851)
- [Compulsory attendance](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741378)
- [Recommended path of study](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418)
- [Point allocation system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408)
- [Preference system](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741408)
- [Waiting list and allocation of remaining places](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741429)

With the point allocation system, you receive a certain number of points (= points account) every semester which you can use for the registration. You will have a points account for the registration for courses and another one for the registration for exams.

If you register for courses offered by different directorates of studies, you will have several points accounts. The points can be used for courses with a limited number of participants.

The more points you allocate to a course, the more likely it is that you are accepted.

The allocation of points can still be changed within the [registration deadline](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741417). You cannot save points for the following semester, which means that you will lose points that you have not used.

You can find further information in the [ABC of terminology.](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741407)

#

title: Registration for courses with continuous assessment
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Registration for courses with continuous assessment
text:
Every directorate of studies (in German: Studienprogrammleitung, SPL) determines for its degree programme how places for courses with a limited number of participants and exams are allocated in its degree programmes.

With the preference system, several courses are offered for the same [curriculum items](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/abc-of-terminology/#c279949). Students rank their registrations for courses according to their personal preference (from top to bottom). The system intends to achieve the highest possible preference. The higher the preference, the more likely it is to get a place in the prefered option.

After the end of the [registration deadline](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/abc-of-terminology/#c298839), the available places are allocated. On average, 98% of all students receive a suitable course place.

You can find further information in the [ABC of terminology](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c273298).

#

The higher the preference, the more likely it is to get a place in the prefered option.

After the end of the [registration deadline](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/abc-of-terminology/#c298839), the available places are allocated. On average, 98% of all students receive a suitable course place.

You can find further information in the [ABC of terminology](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c273298).

title: Registration for courses with continuous assessment
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Registration for courses with continuous assessment
text:
The path of study is a method that the course registration system uses to allocate places in courses of the University of Vienna (in the same manner as the point allocation system or the preference system). Places are allocated according to the recommended path of study: Students who follow this recommended path will more likely get a place.

In the recommended path of study, all courses from the second semester onwards have defined predecessors (for example, the predecessors of courses in the second semester are all courses from the first semester). The degree of completion of these predecessors is indicated in %. If 100 % is indicated next to a module, you have successfully completed all courses from the previous semesters.

Therefore, the information “Path of study: x %” in the record of examinations and the semester planner indicates the degree of completion of courses of the previous semesters according to the recommended path of study. All registrations for courses with continuous assessment are ranked according to this percentage. The higher your percentage, the better your chances to get a place in the course.

You can find further information in the [ABC of terminology](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418).

#
embed_text:
Study organisation > Registration for courses with continuous assessment > Registration steps > Recommended path of study

The path of study is a method that the course registration system uses to allocate places in courses of the University of Vienna (in the same manner as the point allocation system or the preference system). Places are allocated according to the recommended path of study: Students who follow this recommended path will more likely get a place.

In the recommended path of study, all courses from the second semester onwards have defined predecessors (for example, the predecessors of courses in the second semester are all courses from the first semester). The degree of completion of these predecessors is indicated in %. If 100 % is indicated next to a module, you have successfully completed all courses from the previous semesters.

Therefore, the information “Path of study: x %” in the record of examinations and the semester planner indicates the degree of completion of courses of the previous semesters according to the recommended path of study. All registrations for courses with continuous assessment are ranked according to this percentage. The higher your percentage, the better your chances to get a place in the course.

You can find further information in the [ABC of terminology](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c741418).

title: The academic year
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text:
The academic year is divided into winter semester and summer semester. Each semester consists of a period during which courses are held and a lecuture-free period ("holidays").

- The University of Vienna is closed on [public holidays](https://www.wien.gv.at/amtshelfer/feiertage/).
- [Application and admission periods for degree programmes without an entrance exam procedure](/en/admission/application-and-admission-periods/)
- [Application and admission period for degree programmes with an entrance exam procedure](/en/entrance-exam/deadlines-for-entrance-exam-procedures/)

title: Using AI in Your Studies
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text: Imagine you are tasked with writing an academic paper. You are aware of the importance of a clear research question, thorough research and a coherent line of argument. Yet instead of doing everything yourself, there is a new option: AI-supported tools can suggest literature, generate outlines, draft opening sentences and improve your writing style. Artificial intelligence has long become part of everyday student life. At the same time, its use in degree programmes raises important questions: What is permitted? Where are the boundaries? And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
text:
Artificial intelligence refers to computer-based systems that technically simulate certain human cognitive and linguistic abilities. These systems can generate texts, images or other content, but they do not understand these themselves and do not produce knowledge of their own. The use of AI systems also entails risks.

For degree programmes and academic work, this means that AI can provide support, but it does not replace independent thinking, critical evaluation or personal responsibility.

#

Artificial intelligence is commonly understood to refer to computer-based systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence. These include pattern recognition, language processing, decision-making and adapting to new situations. However, such systems are not truly "intelligent" in the sense in which intelligence is generally understood. They generate output solely on the basis of probabilities derived from linguistic patterns. This means:

- AI systems do not understand content, but calculate probabilities.
- They cannot make moral or ethical judgements.
- They do not possess emotional or social intelligence.

Therefore, while they may imitate human intelligence, they do not possess it themselves.

Modern AI systems work on the basis of data. They analyse large amounts of sample data and learn statistical correlations from it. On this basis, they make predictions or generate results that appear meaningful or plausible at first glance.

Responsibility for the use, interpretation and evaluation of AI results therefore always lies with humans.

For degree programmes and academic work, this means that AI can provide support, but it does not replace independent thinking, critical evaluation or personal responsibility.

#

Artificial intelligence is commonly understood to refer to computer-based systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence. These include pattern recognition, language processing, decision-making and adapting to new situations. However, such systems are not truly "intelligent" in the sense in which intelligence is generally understood. They generate output solely on the basis of probabilities derived from linguistic patterns. This means:

- AI systems do not understand content, but calculate probabilities.
- They cannot make moral or ethical judgements.
- They do not possess emotional or social intelligence.

Therefore, while they may imitate human intelligence, they do not possess it themselves.

Modern AI systems work on the basis of data. They analyse large amounts of sample data and learn statistical correlations from it. On this basis, they make predictions or generate results that appear meaningful or plausible at first glance.

Responsibility for the use, interpretation and evaluation of AI results therefore always lies with humans.

title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
heading_path: Two basic types of AI models
heading_paths: Two basic types of AI models
text:
In artificial intelligence, there are two basic types of models, to put it simply:

- Discriminative AI recognises patterns in existing data and assigns new inputs to known categories or values. Typical applications include spam filters and image recognition. Such systems learn to recognise distinguishing features and make decisions based on existing classes.
- Generative AI goes one step further: it generates new content by combining patterns, structures and probabilities from training data. This results in texts, images, music or videos that can appear to be human-created content.

Generative AI models have been "trained" with large amounts of data. This means that they have derived patterns, structures and statistical relationships between words from a vast amount of data, which they use to generate new data on this basis. Depending on the type of data that generative models produce, a distinction is made between language models, image models, audio models and video models, for example.
embed_text:
Study organisation > What is Artificial Intelligence? > How do AI systems work? > Two basic types of AI models

In artificial intelligence, there are two basic types of models, to put it simply:

- Discriminative AI recognises patterns in existing data and assigns new inputs to known categories or values. Typical applications include spam filters and image recognition. Such systems learn to recognise distinguishing features and make decisions based on existing classes.
- Generative AI goes one step further: it generates new content by combining patterns, structures and probabilities from training data. This results in texts, images, music or videos that can appear to be human-created content.

Generative AI models have been "trained" with large amounts of data. This means that they have derived patterns, structures and statistical relationships between words from a vast amount of data, which they use to generate new data on this basis. Depending on the type of data that generative models produce, a distinction is made between language models, image models, audio models and video models, for example.

title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
heading_paths: Risks associated with the use of AI
text:
A widespread form of generative AI are large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT, which specialise in processing human language. They can generate text by calculating which word or sentence fragment is most likely to follow next in a given context.

This means that an LLM has no consciousness and no real understanding of content. In general, it only answers this question:**„Based on the probabilites I have learned, what is the most likely continuation of this text?**

The model therefore does not recognise "meaning" in the human sense, but only:

- statistical correlations between words
- typical sentence structures
- typical argumentation patterns
- typical answers to typical questions

The model does not understand, but the learned patterns and probabilities are so good that the result appears to be understanding.

This does not generate any new knowledge: LLMs combine existing linguistic patterns to create new texts. They do not understand content, reflect on it critically or conduct their own research. Their output is based solely on statistical correlations between words.

The use of AI tools in academic studies also carries risks based on how large language models work.

title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
heading_paths: Risks associated with the use of AI
text:
Sometimes generative AI models produce texts that sound very plausible but are factually incorrect. It hallucinates. This happens because the model

- does not have a genuine internally stored database
- does not distinguish between "sounds plausible" and "is verifiably true"

The system's sole function is to optimise responses to prompts by identifying the most linguistcally probable response.

Example:

If you request a source or quotation and the model has seen many responses during training that follow the pattern:  „The study by XY (2021) shows that ...", then it constructs something along these lines, even if the specific study has never existed.

- does not have a genuine internally stored database
- does not distinguish between "sounds plausible" and "is verifiably true"

The system's sole function is to optimise responses to prompts by identifying the most linguistcally probable response.

Example:

If you request a source or quotation and the model has seen many responses during training that follow the pattern:  „The study by XY (2021) shows that ...", then it constructs something along these lines, even if the specific study has never existed.

title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
heading_paths: Risks associated with the use of AI
text:
AI models are designed to learn the probabilities on which their answers are based from the training data to which they are exposed. Should the data in question contain

- perspectives that are overrepresented,
- groups that are portrayed stereotypically,
- or terms that frequently appear in the same context,

the model will, then, "adopt" these patterns without realising that they are problematic.

Example**:**

If the term "professor" is frequently associated with male forms/examples in the training data, the model is more likely to interpret the term in the same way.

This has implications for the reliability of the results. While AI-generated texts can appear linguistically convincing, they may contain factual errors or reproduce biases from the training data. Large Language models are unable to assess the accuracy of their statements, as they lack the understanding to comprehend content and do not access a verifiable collection of knowledge. They have just learned patterns from large amounts of text during training.

AI models are designed to learn the probabilities on which their answers are based from the training data to which they are exposed. Should the data in question contain

- perspectives that are overrepresented,
- groups that are portrayed stereotypically,
- or terms that frequently appear in the same context,

the model will, then, "adopt" these patterns without realising that they are problematic.

Example**:**

If the term "professor" is frequently associated with male forms/examples in the training data, the model is more likely to interpret the term in the same way.

This has implications for the reliability of the results. While AI-generated texts can appear linguistically convincing, they may contain factual errors or reproduce biases from the training data. Large Language models are unable to assess the accuracy of their statements, as they lack the understanding to comprehend content and do not access a verifiable collection of knowledge. They have just learned patterns from large amounts of text during training.

title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
heading_paths: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

title: AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
heading_paths: Position of the University of Vienna
text:
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

title: Basic framework for AI use
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
text:
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

text: In degree programmes at the University of Vienna, courses and examinations pursue clearly defined learning outcomes. These are designed to develop subject-specific competences and to foster academic work. This includes understanding and accurately presenting content, applying knowledge to one’s own research questions and developing independent arguments, structuring texts coherently and using sources appropriately. The knowledge and skills required to achieve the learning outcomes must be developed independently. If AI assumes these tasks, the learning outcomes cannot be achieved. The competences associated with the learning outcomes are demonstrated in various course and examination formats, for example in examinations, presentations or written assignments. text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

title: What is permitted? And what is not?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
What is permitted? And what is not?
text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

title: What is permitted? And what is not?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
What is permitted? And what is not?
text:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

title: Legal requirements
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
Legal requirements: Data protection and copyright
heading_path: Copyright: Protection of intellectual property and AI-generated content
heading_paths: Copyright: Protection of intellectual property and AI-generated content
text: Copyright is vital for protecting the intellectual creations of authors and ensuring that they have the autonomy to determine how their works are used. - In accordance with prevailing legal principles, the authorship of a work is recognised as belonging exclusively to human beings and not to AI systems. - Although AI is not granted its own copyright to a text, AI-generated content may not be presented as one's own academic work (this is also prohibited by the terms of use of AI tools). Please be aware that uploading your own or third-party texts to AI tools can also present challenges. This is due to the fact that it may involve disclosing copyright-protected content without having control over how this material will be further processed by the tools. - If the AI tool does not use the uploaded texts for training purposes, uploading them should fall under the right to private copying ([Section 42 of the German Copyright Act (UrhG)](https://www.ris.bka.gv.at/GeltendeFassung.wxe?Abfrage=Bundesnormen&Gesetzesnummer=10001848)). - However, it is not permitted to upload entire books or magazines to AI tools. - Material that has obviously been produced in an unlawful manner may also not be uploaded to AI tools. - You must not circumvent any technical copy protection measures. Therefore, please note that you may only upload copyright-protected material to an AI tool for private copying purposes if the data is not used to train

title: Things AI is not recommended for
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
Things AI is not recommended for
text:
While AI has demonstrated remarkable capabilities, there are areas where its use is not recommended, as it could potentially compromise the autonomy of your scientific work or the integrity of your learning processes.

AI-generated summaries can provide an overview, but it is important to remember that they do not replace your own engagement with the material. Please note that AI-generated summaries are often also inadequate in terms of content, as they may omit essential aspects or distort statements from the original.

Recommendation: Engage with the topic yourself first or read the texts and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions, for example to clarify aspects you have not understood.

Tip: Do not use AI as a substitute for reading, but as a reading coach.

AI can support you in reading academic texts actively, critically and in a structured manner. For instance, it can provide guiding questions, suggestions for highlighting central arguments, or explanations of individual terms. It is recommended that you read the text yourself first and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions. This will help you to close gaps in understanding or to better contextualise argumentative structures (see tips on [prompting](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)).

Further information: [AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

title: Things AI is not recommended for
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
Things AI is not recommended for
heading_paths: Tasks for which AI simply is not the right choice
text:
In tasks where precision is paramount, it is recommended to employ software that operates in a deterministic manner, as opposed to AI models that are probabilistic in nature. Traditional software often proves to be the better choice in certain domains, for example for precise data calculations. For this purpose, you may want to use software such as Excel or SPSS, in addition to other data analysis tools available to you under a [campus licence](http://For).

There are areas in which AI performs surprisingly poorly, such as counting or carrying out highly complex tasks. It is therefore important to recognise the limitations of AI and not to use it for purposes for which it is not designed.

**Example: Bibliography**

Do not use AI to generate a bibliography or to arrange an existing one alphabetically. AI may add content or fabricate sources that do not exist. Instead, use a reference management software tool such as Citavi or EndNote, which are available to you with a [campus licence](http://Do).

title: Things AI is not recommended for
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
AI in Your Studies: What is permitted?
Things AI is not recommended for
heading_path: When it is the journey that matters
heading_paths: When it is the journey that matters
text:
There are tasks that require your personal attention to ensure genuine progress. This is particularly relevant in the context of academic writing, where the process of articulating ideas through writing often clarifies their intended expression. The writing process facilitates the organisation of ideas and their refinement into a coherent structure.

The ability to write is developed through practical experience. This process cannot be delegated to artificial intelligence. Mastering the writing process independently is not only essential, but also a fundamental learning outcome of academic work and a key requirement of good academic practice.

There are, however, ways to [support your writing process with AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/).

title: Academic work and writing with AI
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
AI can provide valuable support in academic work and writing by generating ideas, structuring texts, revising language and style, organising literature, and clarifying questions of understanding. However, it is important to note that core academic tasks such as developing research questions, constructing arguments, interpreting, and evaluating must be carried out independently.

It is imperative that the use of AI is aligned with the learning outcomes, subjected to critical reflection, and made transparent. Responsibility for content, quality and academic integrity remains with you.

title: Questions instead of answers: The Socratic Dialogue
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
Questions instead of answers: The Socratic Dialogue
heading_path: Questions instead of answers: The Socratic dialogue
heading_paths: Questions instead of answers: The Socratic dialogue
text:
A proven method for fostering independence in academic thinking is the Socratic dialogue. In this approach, AI does not simply generate content, but poses targeted questions through which you can reflect on your own arguments, identify weaknesses and develop new perspectives. AI tools act as partners in reflection, for example by presenting different viewpoints and prompting critical reflection.

Instead of relying solely on AI-generated content, you maintain control over the development of your argument while working on research questions, argumentative structures and the interpretation of results with AI support, yet independently.

title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
heading_path: Example prompt: Your AI Tutor for academic writing
heading_paths: Example prompt: Your AI Tutor for academic writing
text: “You are an academic AI tutor who supports me in writing my [seminar paper … on the topic of …]. Your task is to provide targeted support depending on the type of task, while respecting the principles of good academic practice and safeguarding my independence and originality. You must also strictly observe the learning outcomes. Distinguish between the following types of tasks: 1. Linguistic, formal and organisational tasks (Later Order Concerns – LOCs): - You support me with tasks relating to linguistic and formal quality as well as organisation, such as revising texts (spelling, grammar, style) or managing literature. - Ensure that your suggestions are clear, precise and helpful, but do not distort the academic context. - Examples: “Can you check this text for grammar and style?” or “Create a list of correctly formatted references from the following sources: […]”. 2. Intellectually demanding tasks (Higher Order Concerns – HOCs): - For tasks such as developing arguments, formulating research questions or interpreting results, you may act only in a supportive capacity. - Your role is that of a partner in reflection in the sense of a Socratic dialogue: you ask targeted, open-ended questions that encourage me to critically examine, further develop and independently deepen my ideas. - Do not provide direct answers or ready-made solutions, but promote my own intellectual contribution. - Examples: “Which assumptions underpin your hypothesis?” or “How might

title: Good Scientific Practice
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
heading_paths: A concise overview of good scientific practice
text:
The use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes is fundamentally guided by the existing principles of good academic practice. These apply irrespective of whether digital tools, traditional resources or AI-supported systems are used.

All scientific work is based on good scientific practice, which ensures that scientific achievements are carried out independently, transparently and honestly.

Key principles of good scientific practice

- Independence: Academic achievements must be accomplished independently.
- Honesty: Other people's contributions – including those made by AI – must not be passed off as your own work.
- Transparency: The use of aids must be disclosed
- Traceability: The process of creating scientific work must remain verifiable.

The principles of good scientific practice also apply without restriction to the use of artificial intelligence and form the basis for dealing with AI in scientific work at the University of Vienna. In addition, you must also respect copyright and data protection regulations.

title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text: For a text produced with the assistance of AI to qualify as your own work, there must be a substantial human contribution that decisively shapes the form of the generated text. In this case, the AI tool merely serves in a supportive capacity as an executing instrument. This applies: - where a highly sophisticated prompt is used, which you have created yourself and whose formulation may require relevant knowledge; - where text is produced through the combination of strongly directive prompts that determine the essential structural decisions rather than leaving them to the AI tool; - where users further develop the generated text as a stimulus for reflection (cf. [Salden und Leschke 2023](https://hss-opus.ub.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/opus4/frontdoor/deliver/index/docId/9734/file/2023_03_06_Didaktik_Recht_KI_Hochschulbildung.pdf)) In these cases, AI complements the academic working process without replacing your independent contribution. What is crucial is that the substantive decisions remain yours. This is the case, for example, when you use AI: - to generate ideas or as a stimulus for reflection; - to obtain structural or outline suggestions that form the basis for further development (not for direct 1:1 adoption); - as a tool for reflection, for example to review arguments; - or to revise the language of your own texts. It is imperative that you critically examine, develop and embed AI-generated results within your own line of argument. The more actively you guide AI and the more decisions you take yourself, the clearer

title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
heading_path: Controlling AI in a targeted manner and maintaining independence
heading_paths: Controlling AI in a targeted manner and maintaining independence
Controlling AI in a targeted manner and maintaining independence
Prompt-Tipp: So formulieren Sie Prompts, die Ihre Eigenleistung sichern
text:
The quality of AI-generated output depends to a large extent on how precisely and reflectively a request is formulated. AI tools do not independently respond to academic requirements; rather, they orient themselves to the specifications they receive through [prompts](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/).

It is important to define the objective, context and framework conditions clearly, as this will ensure the most effective use of AI as a supportive tool. Through targeted prompting, the user retains control over the working process, determines the direction and depth of responses, and ensures that substantive decisions remain theirs. AI thus provides impulses – the academic contextualisation and further development remain the responsibility of the user.

Formulieren Sie Prompts so, dass die KI **nicht für Sie denkt oder schreibt**, sondern Sie beim eigenen Denken unterstützt. Bitten Sie um **Fragen, Alternativen, Rückmeldungen oder Strukturvorschläge**, nicht um fertige Texte oder inhaltliche Entscheidungen. Bewährt haben sich Prompts, die:

- die KI auf eine **unterstützende Rolle** festlegen (zB Feedback, Reflexionshilfe),
- klar benennen, **was die KI nicht tun soll** (zB keine fertigen Argumente),
- deutlich machen, dass **Auswahl, Bewertung und Weiterverarbeitung** bei Ihnen liegen.

**Dabei gilt:** Die Vorschläge dienen als Ausgangspunkt und müssen fachlich geprüft, eingeordnet und weiterentwickelt werden.

title: AI in academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
Academic writing is a skill that you will develop progressively throughout your studies. AI tools can provide support; however, it is crucial that that your independent contribution, the traceability of your work and academic integrity are preserved.

The academic writing process is a thought process. While AI can provide support, it is important to exercise your own judgment and evaluate the outcomes.

[#### Why is academic writing important anyway?](#c1268673)

[#### Use Cases of AI in academic writing](#c1268690)

title: AI in academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
Academic writing is a crucial component of degree programmes, as it demonstrates an individual's ability to understand, contextualise and further develop subject-specific content. The finished text makes visible an individual's capacity to present coherent arguments, handle sources correctly and apply the disciplinary language of their field of study.

However, it is important to note that the final product, i.e. the completed text, is not the only decisive factor. The writing process itself is an essential learning tool: By formulating, structuring and revising, you develop your academic thinking, refine concepts and make reasoned decisions. This is precisely where the central learning gain of academic work lies.

The process of writing allows for the generation of new ideas, the refinement of arguments, and the enhancement of connections between ideas. The ambiguity of one's own line of argument often becomes evident only when attempting to express it in language. Errors and revisions should not be seen as indications of failure, but rather as integral components of the learning process. Each revision contributes to the sharpening of concepts, the improvement of argumentative structures, and the enhancement of the clarity of one's position.

As a consequence, academic writing always also supports the development of your own academic skills.

Writing is not only a means of presenting knowledge, but also an essential tool for understanding and critically reflecting on scientific content.

title: AI in academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna

text: In academic writing, a distinction is made between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs). - Higher-order concerns relate to the substantive and conceptual level of a text. These include developing a viable research question, structuring a coherent line of argument or interpreting results. These aspects constitute the core of independent academic work. - Later-order concerns refer to the linguistic and formal presentation of the text. These include grammar, style, readability and correct citation. Beide Ebenen sind für einen gelungenen wissenschaftlichen Text gleichermaßen wichtig; die Unterscheidung bezieht sich jedoch vor allem auf den **Zeitpunkt im Schreibprozess**, da sprachlich-formale Aspekte sinnvollerweise erst dann im Detail überarbeitet werden, wenn die inhaltlich-konzeptionelle Struktur des Textes tragfähig ausgearbeitet ist. Both levels are equally important for a successful academic text; however, the distinction primarily concerns the stage within the writing process when these tasks are addressed: linguistic and formal aspects are most effectively revised in detail once the substantive and conceptual structure of the text has been developed. title: AI in academic writing
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
heading_paths: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Caution when using AI for literature research
text:
AI tools can support you at different stages of academic writing. It is important to use AI as a tool within the writing process and not as a substitute for your own subject-specific engagement. How AI may support your writing depends on the stage of writing you are currently in.

Important: Do not use generative AI tools such as ChatGPT or u:ai for research. These tools do not access databases and may fabricate sources. Instead, use dedicated literature research tools such as Elicit, Consensus or others.

The library offers specialised training sessions on [literature research with AI](http://urise.univie.ac.at/course/index.php?categoryid=6&wbtsearch=ai+literacy).

text: Write your prompts in a way that AI does not think or write for you, but supports you in your own thinking. Ask for questions, alternatives, feedback or suggestions for structure, rather than for completed texts or substantive decisions. Effective prompts typically: - assign AI a clearly supportive role (for example feedback or assistance in reflection), - explicitly state what AI should not do (for example provide ready-made arguments), - make it clear that selection, evaluation and further development remain your responsibility. It is important that you regard the suggestions as a starting point and review, contextualise and further develop them. # / - Before writing: Orientation and preparation In the early stages of a writing project, AI can help organize thoughts and find initial approaches to a topic. It can provide inspiration, but it cannot replace independent decision-making on content. As a general rule, formulate AI queries in such a way that the AI highlights possibilities and provides feedback, while you remain responsible for selection, evaluation, and further development. Possible areas of application: - Generating ideas: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI provides several different thought-provoking ideas without evaluating or selecting content. Ask for brief explanations of the respective perspectives, not for detailed arguments or text passages. - Develop and narrow down topic suggestions: Ensure that the AI highlights possible variations and distinctions rather than

Possible areas of application: - Suggestions for outlines or tables of contents: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI shows several possible structural variants and explains their internal logic. Avoid requests for a finished or optimal outline. - Tips for ensuring individual paragraphs are easy to understand: Ask the AI to highlight any ambiguities, long sentence structures, or potential obstacles to comprehension. The request should be aimed at obtaining feedback on readability, not at rephrasing or expanding content. - Feedback on text context or argumentation logic: Structure your request in such a way that the AI highlights breaks, jumps, or missing transitions in the text. Then you can decide for yourself whether and how to accept the suggested corrections. - Word choice and phrasing assistance: AI can assist you with word choice and phrasing. Specifically, AI can help you with suggestions for synonyms, rephrasing, or suggestions for variations of a sentence with different linguistic registers. Structure your query so that the AI suggests alternative expressions or synonyms that are stylistically and technically appropriate. Avoid queries that could lead to simplifications in content or shifts in meaning. The decision on which wording to use remains yours. - Check grammar and punctuation: Ask the AI to identify grammatical errors and punctuation mistakes. title: Citing and labelling AI
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

title: Citing and labelling AI
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna

text: With regard to references within the text, a distinction is made between the literal reproduction of AI-generated content and the collaborative development of content with the help of AI. #### Literal reproduction If AI-generated content is incorporated into the text verbatim, this may be treated as a conventional quotation. Traceability is ensured if, in addition to the quoted text,it is recommended to indicate - the name of the AI-tool and, where applicable, the company behind it - the model used - the year of use - and the prompt that generated the text Example: Text generated by ChatGPT (OpenAI, model o1, 2024. Prompt: “Explain the fundamental principles of good academic practice in one sentence.”) In this case, the prompt documents your contribution by indicating which instruction was given. In practice, however, AI output often is created in an iterative process involving multiple prompts and queries. #### Collaborative development Complete documentation of all individual AI interactions is generally not practical. What is more important is that it is clear how AI was used in principle and that the writer was responsible for controlling, selecting and revising the content. The documentation can, for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to

title: Citing and labelling AI
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

title: Citing and labelling AI
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

title: Prompting correctly
breadcrumb: Studying at the University of Vienna
Academic work and writing with AI
Controlling AI in a targeted manner: the importance of prompting correctly
heading_paths: Rule of Thumb: „Act as if it’s human“
text:
The quality of  AI output depends to a great extent on how precisely and thoughtfully a query is formulated. AI tools do not respond independently to scientific requirements; rather, they are guided by the specifications they receive through prompts.

#

A prompt is the input or instruction you give to an AI to perform a specific task or respond in a desired manner.

This means that AI does not work "on its own", but always responds to what you specify. Think of AI as an extremely powerful assistant with enormous expertise that can work very quickly. However, it is crucial that you clearly formulate what you expect from this assistant. Unclear or vague instructions usually lead to correspondingly imprecise results.

The clarity, structure and thoroughness of a prompt directly impact the quality of the answers provided by the AI.

- The AI-tool independently supplements missing information – based onprobabilities.
- The AI-tool determines all actions that are not explicitly specified by the user.
